title: Needle-felted wolf
subtitle: A standing grey and white merino wolf, approximately 15 cm tall, needle-felted over a wire armature
excerpt: A standing wolf figure in grey and white merino, approximately 15 cm tall, built over a wire armature. The layered pelage is added in short tufts over a grey core body, with a white muzzle, dark mask, and characteristically thick neck ruff.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted animal sculpture technique synthesised from general armature-over-wire practice. Wolf anatomy references standard canid proportions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wire skeleton that supports the roving layers of a needle-felted sculpture, allowing the finished piece to hold a posed position.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, the raw material for needle felting.
A felting needle with small notches along the shaft that catch and tangle wool fibres when pushed into a loose mass, binding them together without water or heat.
The coat of a mammal, including the outer guard hairs and the dense underfur. In needle felting, the pelage effect is built up in short tufts of different colours and textures.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: A needle-felted wolf is built over a wire armature: skeleton first, then a core body of coarse grey

of grey and white merino last. The wolf's proportions are heavier and more compact than a dog: broad chest, thick neck, long legs, and a bushy tail carried low. The white muzzle and dark eye mask are added last with fine merino and a size 40

items.name: Merino roving, charcoal or dark grey (mask markings)

content.text: Cut two lengths of 2 mm aluminium wire: one 40 cm for the spine and legs, one 25 cm for the head and neck extension. Bend the longer wire into a U shape for the four legs (each leg 8 cm), with a 10 cm central section for the spine. Bend a short horizontal loop at the end of each leg to form a foot pad and prevent the wire from pulling through the roving. Use the shorter wire to form the neck curve and muzzle extension.

tightly with Corriedale core wool, binding the wire pairs together and building up the barrel of the chest and the hindquarters. Use a size 36 needle to compact the core wool firmly. The core body should measure approximately 10 cm long and 4 cm in diameter at the broadest point. Leave the lower 3 cm of each leg bare wire at this stage: they will be covered with surface roving later.

Roll a tight ball of grey Corriedale approximately 5 cm in diameter on the foam mat using a size 36 needle. Press one end of the ball slightly to create the muzzle area: wolves have a broader, flatter muzzle than dogs. Attach the head to the neck wire by stabbing roving loops around the join until the head sits securely at a forward-looking angle. Ears are small folded triangles of dark grey merino, needle-felted into position on the crown.

Switch to a size 38 needle. Pull short tufts of mid-grey merino (approximately 3 cm long) and stab them into the core body in overlapping rows, working from the rump forward and from the underline upward. Layer the tufts to create depth. Add a white underside across the belly, the inner legs, and the lower muzzle. Build the neck ruff from longer tufts of mixed grey and white merino, working outward from the neck so the fibres splay slightly.
Step 5: Add facial markings and tail

Switch to a size 40 needle for fine detail. Stab small patches of charcoal merino around the eye area to create the dark mask. Add small amber dots for the irises. A pale grey or white bridge of the nose adds definition. The tail is a cylinder of grey and white merino, approximately 8 cm long and 2 cm wide at the base, needle-felted firmly and attached at the rump wire, angled downward slightly.

items.problem: The figure sags at the spine and will not stand
items.cause: The wire armature is too thin or the roving over the spine is insufficiently compacted
items.fix: Add a second wire alongside the spine and compact the covering roving more firmly with a size 36 needle before adding surface layers.

items.problem: The leg surface roving slides up the wire when the figure is posed
items.cause: The foot-pad loop was not made or the lower leg roving is not compacted tightly enough around the wire
items.fix: Re-wrap the lower legs with roving and use a size 36 needle to compact the fibres tightly around the wire before adding surface layers.

items.problem: The surface pelage looks flat and uniform in colour
items.cause: All surface tufts were the same shade of grey applied at the same depth
items.fix: Add a darker grey or charcoal layer over the topline and a white or pale grey layer to the lower sides. Mixing tufts of different lengths gives more visual depth.

content.text: Trim any loose fibre ends with embroidery scissors. Black glass beads or seed beads can replace the needle-felted eye dots for a sharper finish; stitch them on with a strong thread. The figure can be posed in a standing or mid-trot position while the roving is still slightly compressible. Once the fibres are fully compacted, the pose will hold as set.
